text: Welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. My name is Lalit. Today our
guest is Vikram Mehta from MakeMyTrip. Vikram, tell us what battles you are fighting.
Sure. Thanks Lalit. Pleasure to be here. At MakeMyTrip being India's leading travel
aggregator. We see attacks getting more and more advanced. The adversaries are getting
smarter and they are getting faster. There is a growing need to complement this by something
more than just signature or rule based detection. To possess the ability to detect abnormal behavior
and then respond to these attacks in a timely or semi-automated manner.
So you built a SOC system grounds up. Yes.
Entirely. Could you take us through the architecture, how you have built it, what are the various
components and how they work together. Sure. Absolutely. I think what we see here
is a typical next-gen SOC consists of practically starting with data ingest. Now the challenge
with data ingest is hybrid data sources. So we've got the public cloud and we've got on-premise
data sources. We've got the heart of the next-gen SOC there which is Apache Metron. A big thanks
to the Hortonworks team for presenting this to the community. Post that we have a security
data lake which houses telemetry events over a large period of time. We've got a security
orchestration and response platform on the right-hand side which primarily deals with incident response
and automation. Okay. Let's dig deeper in this. So you have disparate systems. How do you ingest
logs in real time? Sure. So as I was saying, in case of a public
cloud, there are various different systems that need to be integrated with in order to,
you know, pull in telemetry events. For instance, S3, for instance, SNS, SQS, CloudTrail, GuardDuty.
There is a need to integrate with AWS with tools like Apache NIFI that have the possess the capability
to build workflows and then pull the telemetry events into Apache Metron. As for the on-prem
ecosystem, it's a pretty standard syslog or Kafka integration that can be used to pull in
the events directly into Apache Metron. Okay. So you got the logs in Apache Metron.
What are the components within Apache Metron that works on this ingested data?
Sure. So I think the first piece is to be able to convert the standard telemetry log format into a
log format that Apache Metron and downstream systems understand. So we call that, I mean,
we call that parsing. Essentially, it converts a syslog log message or a cloud trail or guard duty log
message into another JSON format that Apache Metron understands. So it becomes a JSON format, a much more
readable format. Now you're doing the parsing and there's also another process that you mentioned
is about enrichment and profiling which is important. The contextual intelligence is important around logs.
How does you achieve that? Absolutely. So I think enrichment is key to, you know, provide contextual information
about an event, right. So let's say there is a blog that contains a source IP address and a destination IP address.
Now a source and destination IP address in itself does not mean much, right. It will be important to understand
where the source originates from, its geolocation, maybe the AS number. As far as the destination is concerned,
in a public cloud environment, IP addresses keep changing, right. It might be important to understand
what that server actually is. Is it a flight server? Is it a hotel server? Is it a payment server?
This entire contextual intelligence is fed into the Apache Metron system which then tags the incoming
telemetry data with this contextual intelligence. So we got real-time events in Apache Metron,
we got the visibility. Now we also have security data lake. Yes. How does these two integrate and how
critical is to build a security data lake? Sure. I think in any SOC environment, it's key to
to store and index telemetry data for a large period of time. That could run into months,
it could run into years. At the scale that we operate at, it becomes extremely important to maintain a
horizontally scalable environment which can be trusted to house such large amounts of information.
We were initially leveraging Apache Metron's backbone which is HDFS to store and index these events. We
migrated from the on-prem HDFS setup onto the serverless security data lake as you can see here. So the
persistent data in Apache Metron from HDFS is picked up by an EMR job which transforms the persistent data in
HDFS into S3. From S3 we build, we leverage Athena to build SQL queries and analytics and you know for various
purposes such as log mining, analytics, forensics and so on. So there is an insight from the data which
is residing in S3 for a longer period of time. Absolutely. Maybe for months. Years. And then you
feed it back to maybe Apache Metron to enrich it further. Absolutely, I think that's a very important
aspect here. So the stream processing layer has the capability to create profiles for only a limited
period of time, right, as infrastructure would allow. The security data lake gives us the flexibility to
generate models or profiles over months long of telemetry information which essentially is a lot
more accurate as opposed to let's say a few minutes of profile information. So that's the feedback loop
that goes from the security data lake back into Apache Metron. So now at this point of time since you
identify an incident, how does your users, your analyst take action or system take action on these events
or incidents? Sure. So I think as important as it is to detect an incident, it's important to respond to
that incident as soon as possible and in many ways and in as many ways possible in an automated or semi-automated
manner. There was a need to build this entire orchestration framework which we call Blitz. It's also
available in our open source GitHub repo. It essentially takes an alert from Metron, tags more
relevant contextual information that can probably not be fed into Apache Metron like reverse DNS like
a who is lookup for instance, presents this entire alert in a single pane of glass to a SOC analyst,
you know probably on his mobile handset or via a Jira integration and hence cutting down the
investigation time, right or the triage time that a SOC analyst would usually spend.
The next step is to respond or mitigate the current threat. There are various different
integrations that we have done with let's say a WAF or a firewall or our internal routers that an
analyst could actually let's say at the click of a button respond to an incident that he receives
by let's say blocking off a source. Okay. All right. So you have also automated incident response.
Yes. To an extent we can say. Yeah. Understood the architecture. Could you just take a couple of use
case or one use case that you're solving with this? Sure. I think that's probably the most interesting part
and my favorite topic too. I think the whole concept of an external SOC is to be able to detect unusual
behavior, deviations from normal activity, right. So let's talk about a use case where we are trying
to detect server compromise that has actually bypassed all security, you know rule based or
signature based environment or protection that one might have. A server has a typical characteristic,
let's say a web server would have a certain egress and ingress ratio which is which we call producer
consumer ratio and when profiled over a period of time a typical web server would have a certain
pattern of normal PCR, normal PCR range. Now, if that server starts doing something that it is not
supposed to do, for example, if it gets compromised that might skew the PCR ratio to an extent which is
a deviation from normal behavior and which is what can be detected by a setup like this. So you're
detecting the unknown? Yes, absolutely. Okay. And then what is the scale of this architecture? How many
events you're handling? So I think from like various different data sources, we've probably got around
I think 20 data sources operating at the moment, add up at about 5000 events per second. And as far as
the security data lake is concerned, we index about 250 million events per day. Okay. All right. And now
since you got the data in data lake, what are your next steps? What next you're doing on this platform?
So I think as the next step we primarily, you know, feel we like to leverage the security data lake and
large scale, large amounts of data available to us to carry out threat hunting, to detect even more than
what we are doing today. Thanks Vikram. Thanks for the insight. Pleasure is all mine.
Thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
